output:
**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.**

The New Physics Learning Machine (NPLM) has made learned likelihood-ratio testing a practical engine for model-independent new-physics searches and automated Data Quality Monitoring at the LHC. It compresses an entire experiment into a single Neyman-Pearson statistic t = 2 max_w[log-likelihood-ratio] and a toy-calibrated p-value p(t|R) measured against one reference sample R. That elegance hides a confound: a high t is read as data-versus-null mismatch -- real new physics or a real detector fault -- but the reference itself can be imperfect along three axes: finite-sample fluctuation, silent contamination of the good-run or Monte-Carlo reference by the very anomalies under test, and legitimate non-stationary drift (detector aging, recalibration, pile-up change). The unbalanced N_R >> N_D limit only suppresses fluctuation, and worse, the toys that calibrate p(t|R) are resampled from the same suspect reference, so the bias is self-concealing. We argue t must be promoted to a reference-marginalized, source-attributed goodness-of-fit profile (RC-GoF) that separates data-attributable from reference-attributable mismatch and reports their interaction as a third labeled component -- while provably collapsing back to the standard NPLM scalar t under a clean, stationary, uncontaminated singleton reference, so it strictly generalizes rather than competes.

---

**Here are the five core questions:**

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can scoring a New Physics Learning Machine goodness-of-fit test's anomaly verdict with the single Neyman-Pearson statistic t = 2 max_w [log-likelihood-ratio] and its toy-calibrated p-value p(t|R) against one reference sample conflate genuine data-versus-null mismatch -- real new physics or a real detector fault -- with reference-set imperfection comprising finite-sample fluctuation, silent contamination of the good-run or Monte-Carlo reference by the very anomalies under test, and legitimate non-stationary drift such as detector aging, recalibration, or pile-up change, an entanglement that the unbalanced N_R >> N_D limit cannot remove because the toy datasets that calibrate p(t|R) are resampled from that same imperfect reference, so that t must be promoted to a reference-marginalized, source-attributed goodness-of-fit profile that separates data-attributable mismatch from reference-attributable mismatch and reports their interaction as a third labeled component, and provably collapses back to the standard NPLM scalar t under a clean, stationary, uncontaminated singleton reference?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Learned-likelihood-ratio and two-sample goodness-of-fit (GoF) tests are fast becoming the standard tool for model-independent new-physics searches and for automated Data Quality Monitoring (DQM) at the LHC. The New Physics Learning Machine (NPLM) of D'Agnolo and Wulzer (2019) and D'Agnolo, Grosso, Pierini, Wulzer and Zanetti (2021), together with its kernel/Falkon variant (Grosso et al.), distils an entire experiment into a single Neyman-Pearson statistic t = 2 max_w[log-likelihood-ratio] and a toy-calibrated p-value p(t|R). The same machinery is spreading beyond collider physics -- to astroparticle and neutrino analyses and to the validation of generative surrogate models in HEP. Because one number drives a binary verdict, any silent fusion of real anomaly with reference imperfection has outsized consequences.

The stakes are two-sided. A contaminated or drifted reference inflates t, manufacturing phantom "new physics" or phantom detector faults: false discovery, operator alarm fatigue, and wasted shift-crew investigations during data taking. Conversely, contamination that happens to mimic the signal deflates sensitivity, producing missed discovery -- the worst outcome for a discovery instrument. The unbalanced N_R >> N_D limit that the intro leans on suppresses only finite-sample fluctuation; it is silent on contamination and on legitimate non-stationary drift (detector aging, recalibration, pile-up change), and worse, the toys that calibrate p(t|R) are resampled from that very reference, so the bias is invisible to the standard procedure.

A source-attributed GoF profile changes practice qualitatively. It turns a pass/fail alarm into an actionable diagnosis: "this excess is reference-attributable -- recalibrate or refresh the good-run set," versus "this is data-attributable -- escalate to a physics investigation." For automated DQM at CMS and ATLAS, that distinction is the difference between a trustworthy trip-wire and a noise generator.

The contribution generalizes. The reference-attribution layer is method-agnostic across the entire family of classifier-based and likelihood-ratio two-sample tests -- classifier two-sample tests (Lopez-Paz and Oquab 2017), semi-supervised anomaly tests (Chakravarti et al. 2021), and the weak/anomaly-detection ecosystem (CWoLa, ANODE, CATHODE) -- because all of them implicitly trust a reference the field currently assumes away. Because the profile collapses by construction to the standard NPLM scalar t under a clean, stationary, uncontaminated singleton reference (as recomputed, not claimed as exact reproduction of any published number), it is strictly a generalization, never a competitor. The practical payoff is trustworthy automated monitoring, fewer false alarms, and calibration that no longer silently inherits the reference's own bias.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The core technical obstacle is identifiability: under a single, self-calibrating reference, data-attributable mismatch and reference-attributable mismatch inflate the same scalar t in indistinguishable ways. Four naive fixes each fail for a concrete, separable reason.

First, "use a bigger reference." Increasing N_R drives down only axis (i), the finite-sample fluctuation of the reference histogram/empirical density, because that term scales as 1/N_R. It does nothing to contamination (axis ii) or drift (axis iii): a good-run or Monte-Carlo reference that silently contains the very signal under test, or that was taken under a different detector calibration/pile-up regime, is biased no matter how many events it has. More data makes the wrong reference more precisely wrong, sharpening p(t|R) around a displaced mean.

Second, "detect imperfection from t itself." This is circular. The null p(t|R) is built from TOY datasets resampled from the same reference R. If R is contaminated or drifted, every toy inherits that bias, so the calibrated p-value looks healthy while being systematically wrong (the Aurora calibration-circularity analog). The statistic cannot audit the object that calibrates it.

Third, "compare against another reference." A cross-reference discrepancy mixes genuine reference imperfection with real physics/domain difference between the two samples; it therefore yields only an UPPER BOUND on reference-attributable inflation (Pattern l), never a clean attribution.

Fourth, and most fundamental, is a sign non-identifiability. A deficit in the reference and an excess in the data both push t upward with the same sign: the log-density-ratio f_w(x)=log[p_data/p_ref] rises whether p_ref is too low or p_data is genuinely too high. Without extra structure - controlled injection of known contamination, an external clean anchor, or assumptions bounding the contamination fraction epsilon and the smoothness of the drift operator - no amount of fitting can apportion t between the two.

Compounding all of this, the NPLM optimum f_w is a high-dimensional non-convex object (NPLM-NN) or an RKHS minimizer over a large kernel system (NPLM-kernel/Falkon). Propagating reference-imperfection variance through that optimizer into clean, labeled source-attributed components - rather than letting it leak into the data-attributable term - is statistically delicate: the estimator is nonlinear in the reference, so reference perturbations do not map linearly onto changes in t. The hard part is not building a richer test; it is breaking the self-calibration loop and recovering identifiability of data-attributable versus reference-attributable inflation from a single reference that is simultaneously the thing under test and the thing that calibrates the test.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work assumes the problem away. The NPLM papers (D'Agnolo and Wulzer 2019; D'Agnolo, Grosso, Pierini, Wulzer and Zanetti 2021; Grosso et al.) treat the reference R as a fixed, clean null. They bound finite-sample fluctuation through the N_R >> N_D argument and calibrate p(t|R) with toy datasets, but they do not model contamination of the good-run or Monte-Carlo reference by the very anomalies under test, and they do not model legitimate non-stationary drift. Crucially, they never separate reference-attributable from data-attributable inflation of t. The broader GoF literature shares the assumption: classifier two-sample tests (Lopez-Paz and Oquab 2017), unbinned multivariate GoF (Williams 2010; Weisser and Williams 2016), semi-supervised tests (Chakravarti et al. 2021), recursive product-of-spacings (Eller and Shtembari 2023), and the Baker and Cousins (1984) / Neyman and Pearson (1933) foundations all presuppose a trusted null. Robust-statistics and contamination theory exist, but have not been fused with learned-likelihood-ratio GoF in HEP.

Several barriers kept this invisible. First, the N_R >> N_D "weak dependence" folklore created a false sense of safety; because it is literally true for a clean, stationary reference, practitioners read it as a general guarantee. Second, and decisively, calibration circularity: the toys that define p(t|R) are resampled from the same imperfect reference, so a contaminated or drifted reference shifts both the observed t and its null distribution together, hiding the bias from the standard procedure -- this is the NPLM analog of the train/test-share-the-reference confound. Third, there has been no agreed way to inject controlled contamination and drift and then recover a known ground-truth attribution. Fourth, reference imperfection is easily conflated with genuine domain shift, so naive cross-reference comparison only yields an upper bound on the confound, never a clean decomposition.

Our approach differs on four concrete points. (1) It is the first to define the three-axis reference-imperfection decomposition -- finite-sample fluctuation, contamination, drift -- for a learned-likelihood-ratio GoF verdict. (2) It breaks the calibration circularity by calibrating against an external or marginalized null rather than toys drawn from the suspect reference. (3) It provides a by-construction collapse guarantee back to the standard NPLM scalar t under a clean, stationary, uncontaminated singleton reference, so the method strictly generalizes rather than competes (the collapse is verified as recomputed, with cross-reference gaps reported only as an upper bound that conflates confound with domain shift). (4) It supplies an injection-recovery falsification protocol on LHC Olympics 2020 and Dark Machines-style datasets, so attribution claims are testable against planted ground truth rather than asserted.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD - the RC-GoF estimator. (1) Reference-imperfection model: parameterize a contamination fraction epsilon (fraction of data-like/anomalous events that have leaked into the reference) and a drift operator D acting on detector-level features (energy-scale shift, resolution smearing, pile-up-dependent occupancy) that models aging/recalibration. R is thus a point in an imperfection manifold, not a fixed truth. (2) Reference-marginalized null: replace the single toy source with an ENSEMBLE of references spanning the imperfection model - nonparametric bootstrap (axis i), contamination injection at controlled epsilon (axis ii), and drift augmentation via D (axis iii) - and recompute p(t|R) over that ensemble. This breaks the calibration circularity: the null now reflects how much t moves under plausible reference imperfection, not just resampling of one biased reference. (3) Source attribution via a crossed variance-components decomposition of t (and of the per-event field f_w(x)): a data-attributable component (mismatch stable across the reference ensemble), a reference-attributable component (mismatch that tracks epsilon and D), and a labeled INTERACTION (third component - never folded into a main effect). (4) Collapse operator: with epsilon=0, D=identity, and a singleton clean stationary reference, the ensemble degenerates to one toy source, the reference-attributable and interaction components vanish, and RC-GoF returns standard NPLM t EXACTLY (by-construction unit test, within floating-point tolerance, as recomputed - not the published number). (5) Secondary localization axis: project the reference-attributable component back onto features/regions to tell the operator WHERE the reference is wrong (which kinematic bin, which chamber).

BASELINE LADDER. B1 = standard NPLM on a singleton clean reference (the by-construction collapse target). B2 = reference-swap / toy-source placebo with the data fit frozen - under a clean regime it must show approximately no spurious attribution. B3 = full RC-GoF with the contamination+drift-marginalized ensemble, reporting the three source-attributed components. B4 = external clean-anchor oracle (independent control sample or high-statistics MC) = UPPER BOUND ONLY, since it conflates reference imperfection with genuine domain shift (Pattern l).

IMPLEMENTATIONS. Run on BOTH NPLM-NN and NPLM-kernel (Falkon) to show the attribution is implementation-robust.

DATASETS. LHC Olympics 2020 R&D and black-box datasets and the Dark Machines anomaly dataset - both permit CONTROLLED signal injection, giving a ground-truth contamination fraction. A DQM use case (CMS drift-tube chambers good-run vs faulty-run, or a simulated detector-aging/pile-up drift) exercises the drift axis. Toy multivariate Gaussian/mixture benchmarks serve the unit tests.

METRICS. (1) Size/false-positive rate of the test under a contaminated+drifted reference versus nominal - does standard NPLM lose calibration, and does RC-GoF restore nominal size? (2) Attribution accuracy: recovered reference-attributable fraction versus injected ground truth (injection-recovery curve). (3) Power/ROC for a true injected signal at fixed size. (4) Collapse-identity error (must be approximately zero). (5) Localization quality: IoU / rank-correlation of flagged reference-bad features against the injected imperfection.

FALSIFIABILITY. By-construction collapse to B1 (unit test). Two-leg placebo: Leg 1, label-randomized/shuffled reference imperfection must yield approximately no attribution; Leg 2, an absent-confound clean stationary regime must yield approximately no attribution. Injection-recovery monotonicity: the recovered reference-attributable fraction must increase monotonically with injected epsilon and drift magnitude and tend to zero as both tend to zero. If any leg fails, RC-GoF is refuted. Calibration claims are bounded and honest - RC-GoF restores size under the MODELED imperfection class, not under arbitrary shift.

COMPUTE. On the order of a few hundred GPU/CPU-hours: toy unit tests are negligible; NPLM-kernel/Falkon runs are cheap even across the reference ensemble; the NPLM-NN runs are the cost driver (each ensemble member plus each injection point is a fresh max_w fit). A realistic budget is an ensemble of ~200-500 reference draws times ~10-20 injection points across epsilon and drift, dominated by the NN refits.

---

## Background and Current State of the Art

NPLM casts new-physics detection as a Neyman-Pearson hypothesis test learned from data. A flexible model is trained to approximate the extended-likelihood log-density-ratio f_w(x) = log[p_data(x)/p_ref(x)] between an observed dataset and a reference R; the maximized log-likelihood-ratio yields the scalar test statistic t = 2 max_w[log-likelihood-ratio], an Asimov-style measure of data-versus-reference incompatibility. Two implementations dominate: the original neural-network parameterization (NPLM-NN; D'Agnolo and Wulzer 2019; D'Agnolo, Grosso, Pierini, Wulzer, Zanetti 2021) and a kernel/Falkon RKHS variant (Grosso et al. 2021) that is far cheaper to fit. Significance comes from a toy-calibrated null p(t|R): pseudo-experiments are resampled from R to build the t distribution under the no-signal hypothesis. The unbalanced N_R >> N_D limit is invoked so the reference behaves as a fixed null, suppressing its finite-sample fluctuation.

NPLM sits within a wider GoF and two-sample ecosystem: unbinned multivariate GoF via energy/distance statistics (Williams 2010; Weisser and Williams 2016), classifier two-sample tests (Lopez-Paz and Oquab 2017), semi-supervised anomaly tests (Chakravarti, Kuusela, Lei, Wasserman 2021), recursive product-of-spacings (Eller and Shtembari 2023), and the binned-likelihood foundations (Neyman and Pearson 1933; Baker and Cousins 1984). The weak-supervision/anomaly-detection branch -- CWoLa, ANODE, CATHODE -- learns signal-vs-background or density-ratio contrasts directly from data. All share one assumption: a trusted reference or background template. Operationally, NPLM is increasingly proposed for automated Data Quality Monitoring at CMS and ATLAS, where good-run references and Monte-Carlo templates are exactly the objects most prone to contamination and drift.

## Key Findings and Analysis

The central finding is a named, structural gap: the standard NPLM verdict fuses genuine data-versus-null mismatch with reference-set imperfection, and the field currently has no mechanism to separate them. We decompose reference imperfection into three axes -- (i) finite-sample fluctuation, (ii) silent contamination of the good-run or MC reference by the very anomalies under test, and (iii) legitimate non-stationary drift (detector aging, recalibration, pile-up change). The N_R >> N_D argument addresses only axis (i); axes (ii) and (iii) are invariant to reference size and bias t persistently.

The decisive obstacle is a calibration-circularity self-confound: because p(t|R) is built from toys resampled from R, a contaminated or drifted reference shifts both the observed t and its null together. The p-value stays superficially healthy while being systematically wrong; the statistic cannot audit the object that calibrates it. Beneath this lies an identifiability problem with a sign non-identifiability at its core -- a deficit in the reference and an excess in the data both raise f_w(x) and inflate t the same way, so no single-reference fit can apportion t between sources without extra structure (controlled injection, an external anchor, or bounds on epsilon and drift smoothness).

Our resolution, RC-GoF, promotes t to a reference-marginalized, source-attributed profile via a crossed variance-components decomposition into data-attributable, reference-attributable, and a labeled interaction term. Critically, it carries a by-construction collapse guarantee: under epsilon=0, drift operator D=identity, and a clean stationary singleton reference, the reference and interaction components vanish and RC-GoF returns the standard NPLM scalar t exactly (recomputed, to floating-point tolerance). It strictly generalizes the test rather than competing with it.

## Novel Insights and Recommendations

The reusable insight is that reference attribution is a method-agnostic layer, not an NPLM-specific patch. Any learned likelihood-ratio or classifier two-sample test -- NPLM-NN, NPLM-kernel/Falkon, classifier two-sample tests, semi-supervised anomaly tests, and the CWoLa/ANODE/CATHODE family -- implicitly trusts a reference that the field assumes away. Wrapping any of them in a reference-marginalized ensemble plus a crossed variance-components decomposition yields source-attributed verdicts wherever a reference is used.

For DQM practice we recommend a concrete shift: stop reporting pass/fail (or a bare t and p-value) and instead report the source-attributed verdict -- data-attributable mismatch, reference-attributable mismatch, and their interaction. This converts an alarm into an actionable diagnosis ("recalibrate or refresh the good-run set" versus "escalate to a physics investigation"), directly reducing false discovery, alarm fatigue, and wasted shift-crew time.

Two caveats keep the contribution honest. First, calibration is bounded: RC-GoF restores nominal size under the MODELED imperfection class (parameterized contamination epsilon and drift operator D), not under arbitrary domain shift; cross-reference comparisons give only an upper bound on the confound. Second, the secondary localization axis -- projecting the reference-attributable component back onto features and detector regions -- is a practical payoff: it tells operators not just that the reference is wrong but WHERE (which kinematic bin, which chamber), turning attribution into a maintenance signal.

## References and Evidence

[1] Neyman, J. and Pearson, E. S. (1933). On the Problem of the Most Efficient Tests of Statistical Hypotheses. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A. (Foundational hypothesis-testing.)

[2] Baker, S. and Cousins, R. D. (1984). Clarification of the Use of Chi-Square and Likelihood Functions in Fits to Histograms. Nuclear Instruments and Methods in Physics Research. (Binned-likelihood GoF foundation.)

[3] Friedman, J. H. (2003). On Multivariate Goodness-of-Fit and Two-Sample Testing. (Classifier-based two-sample testing precursor.)

[4] Williams, M. (2010). How Good Are Your Fits? Unbinned Multivariate Goodness-of-Fit Tests in High Energy Physics. Journal of Instrumentation.

[5] Weisser, C. and Williams, M. (2016). Machine Learning and Multivariate Goodness of Fit. arXiv:1612.07186.

[6] Lopez-Paz, D. and Oquab, M. (2017). Revisiting Classifier Two-Sample Tests. International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR).

[7] Chakravarti, P., Kuusela, M., Lei, J., and Wasserman, L. (2021). Model-Independent Detection of New Physics Signals Using Interpretable Semi-Supervised Classifier Tests. arXiv:2102.07679.

[8] D'Agnolo, R. T. and Wulzer, A. (2019). Learning New Physics from a Machine. Physical Review D 99, 015014. (Original NPLM.)

[9] D'Agnolo, R. T., Grosso, G., Pierini, M., Wulzer, A., and Zanetti, M. (2021). Learning Multivariate New Physics. European Physical Journal C. (NPLM extended.)

[10] Letizia, M., Grosso, G. et al. (2022). Learning New Physics Efficiently with Nonparametric Methods. European Physical Journal C. (Kernel/Falkon NPLM variant.)

[11] Eller, P. and Shtembari, L. (2023). A Goodness-of-Fit Test Based on a Recursive Product of Spacings.

[12] Krause, C. and Shih, D. (2023). CaloFlow: Fast and Accurate Generation of Calorimeter Showers with Normalizing Flows. Physical Review D. (Generative-surrogate validation context.)

[13] Kansal, R. et al. (2023). Evaluating Generative Models in High Energy Physics. Physical Review D. (Generative-model evaluation context.)

[14] Claeskens, G. and Hjort, N. L. (2004). Goodness-of-Fit via Nonparametric Likelihood Ratios. Scandinavian Journal of Statistics. (Likelihood-ratio GoF theory.)

[15] Alwall, J. et al. (2009). MadGraph: Automated Computation of Tree-Level and Next-to-Leading-Order Differential Cross Sections. (Monte-Carlo reference generation context.)

[16] Metodiev, E. M., Nachman, B., and Thaler, J. (2017). Classification Without Labels: Learning from Mixed Samples in High Energy Physics (CWoLa). Journal of High Energy Physics. (Weak-supervision anomaly method.)

[17] Nachman, B. and Shih, D. (2020). Anomaly Detection with Density Estimation (ANODE). Physical Review D 101, 075042. (Density-ratio anomaly method.)

[18] Hallin, A. et al. (2022). Classifying Anomalies THrough Outer Density Estimation (CATHODE). Physical Review D. (Anomaly-detection method.)

[19] [Dataset/Context] Kasieczka, G., Nachman, B., Shih, D. et al. (2021). The LHC Olympics 2020: A Community Challenge for Anomaly Detection in High Energy Physics. Reports on Progress in Physics. (R&D and black-box datasets; controlled signal injection.)

[20] [Dataset/Context] Aarrestad, T. et al. (2021). The Dark Machines Anomaly Score Challenge: Benchmark Data and Model-Independent Event Classification for the LHC. SciPost Physics. (Anomaly dataset; controlled injection.)

[21] [Context] CMS and ATLAS Collaborations. Data Quality Monitoring Systems and Good-Run Lists. (DQM operational context: good-run vs faulty-run references, drift-tube chambers, pile-up regimes.)
